title: Peer TV South Tyrol — live segment
text: Peritivo time is one of those moments when the world feels a little lighter. Laughing, raising a glass, taking a deep breath. A shared moment with people who matter to us. And here in the South Tyrol, this moment takes on a distinct flavour. Handmade, organic and full of passion. Rondo and Red Veneziano offer South Tyrol in a glass for anyone who loves an aperitif and values authentic drinks. Hello everyone, I am Magdalena Steiner and I'm really pleased to bring you along on my next assignment, a visit to the Valcher Distillery. Here in Apiano, they make some very special aperitifs. They use the best natural ingredients and absolutely no artificial flavourings or colours. Let's go and see for ourselves. Hi Magdalena. Hi Martin. I heard you want to find out a bit about our special aperitifs. Exactly. People tell me they're completely natural and I want to see for myself. Let's go inside then. Wow, you've got everything ready for us here. Yes, making a truly natural aperitif is important to us. We don't use any artificial colours or other tricks in our drinks. Everything is completely natural. This is our rondot, an alcoholic natural aperitif and this is our Veneziano, the non-alcoholic version. So you mentioned pure nature. Does that mean what we see here is what goes inside? Yes, four ingredients from natural and organic farming. This is gentian root which gives the drink depth and a touch of bitterness. Then for that classic bitter taste we have mugwort. Does it have a distinct taste or smell? Yes, you can smell it and the aroma is very bitter. Over here in this basket we have rhubarb root. It provides a very intense flavour and as you can already see this yellow tone gives the drink a real brightness. And does the deep red colour come from the orange? The colour doesn't actually come from the oranges. The oranges are very important because of the strong essential oils in their peel which we can already smell. We press the peels and the essential oils we extract create that bold fruity aroma inside the bottle. But the colour comes from somewhere else. We use an organic fruit concentrate made from apple, carrot and blueberry juice. And that brilliant red is what you then see in your aperitif glass. And are these oranges which are the heart of the drink and provide the main flavour, special in any way? Where do they come from? They ripen in the Sicilian sun. They're picked by hand and grow on the lovely hills around Mount Etna. So it is not just about quality ingredients but also quality work. What happens to these oranges once they arrive here after the harvest? When the oranges get to us the first thing we do is inspect them. If the quality is right we peel them by hand. You have to be careful to avoid the white part because we only want the essential oils. Do you need a lot of peel or? Yes, we need a lot of peel. We start peeling them into this small bucket but then they go into a much larger container. Once we have enough we cover them in alcohol and leave them to macerate. The alcohol draws the essential oils out of the peel and that's how we give our aperitif its fruity aroma. How long do they stay in the container? Our peels macerate in the alcohol for 40 days. After that we press them to extract every last drop of this precious essence. I can show you how it works using this small press. So we press the peels together and what comes out is the essence that gives the drink its aroma and a bit of its colour. This essence is highly aromatic. It gives our aperitif its typical orange note along with a little bit of colour. How many bottles do you produce? We produce between 50,000 and 60,000 bottles of our aperitif. It sounds like a lot but when you consider that people drink over a million aperitifs every day worldwide you realise it's quite a small niche. A small niche created with heart I would say. Above all it is about quality and that is a significant advantage. Peritif is more than a drink. It's a feeling, a pause, a moment to share. This is exactly where Rondo and Red Veneziano reveal their best qualities natural, light and full of South Tyrolian character. In the end it's important to us to make a natural, genuine and high quality drink to enjoy with friends. Well then to a special moment and to natural ingredients. Cheers! The heart of the Alps lies the Isarco Valley the northernmost wine growing region in Italy. Here where alpine peaks and Mediterranean hilly landscapes merge vines have been cultivated for a long time. The steep vineyards along the imposing diorite walls of Saviona are among the best. This holy mountain is one of the oldest pilgrimage sites in the Tyrol and the vineyards themselves are several centuries old. Viticulture in the Isarco Valley thrives at different altitudes where mineralized soils are worked, fertilized and cultivated. I'm Helmut Kürcher and today I'm going to discover the wine and terroir of the Isarco Valley. First I'm going to meet the owner of the Valle Isarco Winery Armin Gratl, one of the highest vineyards in Tiso. Hi Helmut! Hi Armin! Good to be here! Wow! What a location! Quite a sight, an absolute nature! What altitude are we at? Yeah, we are in Tiso at an altitude of 850 meters. What a beautiful shape, arched one might say. Does it have to do with solar radiation? That's right, at this altitude everything is facing south because we need the sun's heat. It's interesting to see more and less light colors. Does it have to do with the variety of vines? Both are cultivated with kernels, but one vineyard is older than the other, hence the difference in foliage color. You can tell there's a lot of work behind it. There are 135 families cultivating this plot and everything is done by hand and within the family. This means a lot of work from early in the morning until the evening. At these altitudes it's mainly white wines that dominate, isn't it? That's right, at the Valle Isarco Winery we have 98% white wines. Here at Altitude it is a cooler area and therefore the focus is clearly on white wines. Of course, also in the quality of the soils. Here we're in the heart of the Dolomites. I assume that minerality plays an important role. Exactly, from here we see the Oddley. So we are right at the foot of the Dolomites where we have very sparse and stony soil but rich in minerals. Behind us we have the Plaza, a mountain from which we get mineral water and I think that's enough to say how mineral rich the soil is. Temperatures also count. The temperature range between day and night is very important here. It is certainly one of the most important things for us. During the day it can be very hot. We have a lot of sun. While at night the cold alpine winds are what gives freshness to our wines. So really crisp fresh wines with character. That's right, it results in really fresh and fruity white wines. We're talking about 2,500 years of wine-growing history in South Tyrol. Here you can feel both nature and history. The Izarko Valley has been the passage from south to north for 2,000 years. The Brenner Pass is the least elevated pass in the Alps and therefore this transit area was already in use 2,000 years ago. As a result of which many castles and monasteries were built and wine-growing began very early. For me this is one of the most important topics, not just drinking a glass of wine but learning about the history of the entire region. That's right, that's what happens in the Izarko Valley. Simply thanks to the ancient farms where the vineyards are still cultivated by hand with the same care as one would devote to one's own garden and are handed down from generation to generation. So really seductive, intense aromas. It's a combination of Riesling and Vernacha and these two varieties that have written history in South Tyrol and you can perceive both. The Vernacha has a sweeter note while the acidity comes more from the Riesling. Right, I believe that Kerner and in particular Kerner Aristos represents the Izarko Valley very well. We're talking about mountain wines with great character and I'm convinced that this is one of the wines that best represents this area. It is indeed an authentic Izarko Valley wine but you also produce other wines. What does Sabiona Monastery represent, for instance? Exactly. If in a pyramid we start at the base we find a classic line with ten varieties of white wines. The Aristos line is produced in the best areas. Then, further up, there are exclusive lines as the Sabiona Monastery wines of which is Sabiona Silvana and the Kerner which grow in the Monastery's own vineyards. In addition, we have had a Kuve since 2020 with four different varieties, which is also in the premium segment. And finally, the newest wine is a wine that takes its structure from within the stone, from granite. And here again, we have a direct reference to our territory. And this is the peculiarity, the uniqueness maturing a wine inside the stone which reflects the mountain itself. This shows that you're in step with the times and that you have a vision. We want to combine tradition with innovation which means continuing everything that was already being done correctly before but also put a new focus on quality with an eye to the future. It's getting warmer and warmer, which gives us the opportunity to develop upwards to grow vines even above 1000 meters and this will guarantee the future of wine growing here in the Izarko Valley. Thank you. Here's to that. Cheers. Cheers. In this modern wine shop, one can taste truly exciting wines, the ambiance is recreated with natural materials such as wood and Bressanone granite, which reflect the characteristics of the Izzaco Valley wine growing area. Our land, south to roll, thanks to its geographical position has so many facets and is an extraordinary wine growing area rich in harmonies and contrasts, bound to traditions but capable of looking to the future. And I'm already thinking of visiting the next winery to get to know this small but wonderful land of wine better, for sure an exciting journey of discovery. Historically, Naturno has a long tradition of thermal springs. The Romans appreciated the energy of the hot springs and treatment centers along their important commercial route, the Via Claudia Augusta. Still today, water from hydro-thermal springs is a powerful tool for health and well-being. Whether it is rheumatism, arthritis or circulatory problems, the hot mineral water provides relief and well-being. At a depth of about 200 meters at the foot of Montessole under Juval Castle, the spring water flows at a temperature of 17 degrees centigrade. It is therefore used for low temperature applications and turns the heated pools of the health spas in Naturno into a true source of youth. The thermal water contains minerals such as sulphur, calcium, magnesium, bicarbonate, sodium and potassium which help relaxation, rejuvenation and invigoration. Focusing on the spring water, Naturno aims to stand out in the future from other towns in the Bur Graviato and South Tyrol in general. We have a long tradition of well-being in Naturno and in this sense the spring water allows us to hold a truly unique position. It's found in pools and hydro massage baths for relaxation but spring water treatments are also available. In addition, it's also offered to drink at some hotels. Spring water has great potential as a benefit to health. First, it relaxes and invigorates, increasing resistance to stress. The thermal bath stimulates circulation and cellular metabolism. The heat of the beneficial water leads to a series of changes in the body in general and especially in the muscles and skeletal system, including pain relief. In collaboration with the University of Pisa, the water was used in a group of subjects over two years and the effects observed and documented. Basically, you could say that it has effects on the muscular system, stress, and this can also be seen in the levels of cortisol. What is also very important is that it has positive effects on muscular and bone disorders such as arthrosis, fibromalgia, rheumatism. Then what I observed during the study is that it supports proper sleep. It's also said to reinforce the immune system, stimulating the body's defences. You could say that it makes the body relax better and it creates a better feeling overall. So basically, the body's vitality is strengthened. Not only guests at the thermal and wellness bars can benefit from the spring water since aquaventura naturno provides this precious resource in two new hydromassage baths where it is possible to enjoy the relaxing effect of the water. Nothing is better than simply immersing yourself in this marvellous thermal water, healthy for the body and spirit. Let's consider a dangerous witch. A herbal expert is a true treasure today. Rosie, a herbalist from the Pasiria Valley, takes us on a guided tour to discover the wealth hidden by nature in the most unexpected places, ending with an excellent tasting. I am Rosie, the herb witch of the valley. Now let's start. We'll walk a short way along the sunwalk and end up at my farm. Rosie, from a long family herbalist tradition, loves to call herself a herb witch. But she is more of a sorceress because what she knows and conveys and what she prepares seem to be the fruit of magic. Instead, it is wise, ancient knowledge that she will give us a taste of on this guided tour through the streets of San Leonardo in Pasiria. What we find along the way is always surprising. The meeting point is the tourism office in the center of San Leonardo and Rosie has already found the first treasure after just a few steps. Now I would like to show you the willow. The willow is a natural aspirin. It is generally gathered in winter when the bark and catkins are found, because these are what are used rather than the leaves. We are not aware of it, but we have a pharmacy outside. This is what we learn almost immediately when the queen of all medicinal plants used in cooking and medicine appears amid some unkempt grass along the street, nettle. A nettle is one of the most important plants we have, as well as one of the oldest in use. It has been used for at least 5,000 years and at least 2,500 species exist. Traversing ancient farmhouses, vegetable and flower gardens, we leave the center for the forest where the discoveries never end, such as the thousand uses and secrets of elder. It is one of the most important agricultural plants we have, and I call it the farmer's vic. It is really incredible in all respects. It heals wounds, it is an anti-inflammatory, it helps the immune system, it is a fever reducer, it favors perspiration and so it is one of the most important plants we have, the only non-poisonous part of elder are the small white flowers without the stem, all the rest of the plant is poisonous. It contains a high dose of hydrogen cyanide. To neutralize this, it is used in alcoholic extracts or as a herbal tea. And there is also a herb used by the Romans and originating in the Mediterranean, the ground elder, whose sap with a scent of wild carrot, celery and parsley is very useful in the kitchen, but also in baths to help inflammation. And now it is time to taste the properties of these herbs. Now we will go to my farm to prepare a herb pesto which you can taste. The last step of this exciting trip is Rosie's farmhouse where she has a very large and rich garden and her shop. Here with a splendid view of the valley and jovial castle, Rosie has arranged everything we need to prepare a surprising pesto of wild herbs. Let's go ladies, it's time to shred. The right mix of herbs mixed with pumpkin seeds, high quality extra virgin olive oil and rums and salt and this pesto on a slice of homemade, naturally leavened bread successfully completes this tour with Rosie. Please ladies. Bike fans get their money's worth in the Ega Valley and have the opportunity to explore one of the most beautiful biking regions in South Tyrol. The bike school Egental organizes guided mountain bike and e-bike tours for every level. For the full biking experience in Val d'Ega, you don't necessarily have to have your own bike. Numerous rentals are available to guests and locals. In addition, everything you could need is available here at Ivan Riegler's bike store in Nova Ponente. I have e-bikes with Bosch motors for rental here. On offer are also fullies or regular bikes to rent and everything else enthusiastic bikers might want. T-shirts, cycling goggles and backpacks are all available here. Since the right equipment has been found, the next step is to choose a suitable tour. In Val d'Ega, hobby and passionate cyclists get their money's worth. The biking region has much to offer. We ride basically through the entire region. The bike guide leads you along the correct trails, so maps are unnecessary. We also provide information on the surrounding mountains and the local terrain. The first group is already ready to go. The bike guide provides information about the tour before the start and the last participants are soon rounded up. The first group goes in the direction of the Costa Lunga pass. The second group has planned a small, more leisurely alpine tour for this hot summer day. Together with two bike guides, they cycle along forest paths and underneath leafy canopies. Again and again, the bikers are rewarded with lovely mountain vistas that come into view. During the ride, the guides always give helpful advice. So that it's not too dull for the younger participants, the trails also lead over more challenging routes and stones. We explain a little bit about how to break, take turns and keep control of the bike. We can offer almost anything here, from trails with a difficulty rating of five and six to courses suited for beginners.

Laughing, raising a glass, taking a deep breath.
A shared moment with people who matter to us.
And here in the South Tyrol, this moment takes on a distinct flavour.
Handmade, organic and full of passion.
Rondo and Red Veneziano offer South Tyrol in a glass for anyone
who loves an aperitif and values authentic drinks.
Hello everyone, I am Magdalena Steiner
and I'm really pleased to bring you along on my next assignment,
a visit to the Valcher Distillery.
Here in Apiano, they make some very special aperitifs.
They use the best natural ingredients
and absolutely no artificial flavourings or colours.
Let's go and see for ourselves.
Hi Magdalena.
Hi Martin.
I heard you want to find out a bit about our special aperitifs.
Exactly. People tell me they're completely natural
and I want to see for myself.
Let's go inside then.
Wow, you've got everything ready for us here.
Yes, making a truly natural aperitif is important to us.

Everything is completely natural.
This is our rondot, an alcoholic natural aperitif
and this is our Veneziano, the non-alcoholic version.
So you mentioned pure nature.
Does that mean what we see here is what goes inside?
Yes, four ingredients from natural and organic farming.
This is gentian root which gives the drink depth
and a touch of bitterness.
Then for that classic bitter taste we have mugwort.
Does it have a distinct taste or smell?
Yes, you can smell it and the aroma is very bitter.
Over here in this basket we have rhubarb root.
It provides a very intense flavour
and as you can already see this yellow tone gives the drink a real brightness.
And does the deep red colour come from the orange?
The colour doesn't actually come from the oranges.
The oranges are very important because of the strong essential oils in their peel
which we can already smell.
We press the peels and the essential oils we extract
create that bold fruity aroma inside the bottle.
But the colour comes from somewhere else.
We use an organic fruit concentrate
made from apple, carrot and blueberry juice.
And that brilliant red is what you then see in your aperitif glass.
And are these oranges which are the heart of the drink
and provide the main flavour, special in any way?
Where do they come from?
They ripen in the Sicilian sun.
They're picked by hand and grow on the lovely hills around Mount Etna.
So it is not just about quality ingredients but also quality work.
What happens to these oranges once they arrive here after the harvest?
When the oranges get to us the first thing we do is inspect them.
If the quality is right we peel them by hand.
You have to be careful to avoid the white part
because we only want the essential oils.
Do you need a lot of peel or?
Yes, we need a lot of peel.
We start peeling them into this small bucket
but then they go into a much larger container.
Once we have enough we cover them in alcohol and leave them to macerate.
The alcohol draws the essential oils out of the peel
and that's how we give our aperitif its fruity aroma.
How long do they stay in the container?
Our peels macerate in the alcohol for 40 days.
After that we press them to extract every last drop of this precious essence.
I can show you how it works using this small press.
So we press the peels together and what comes out is the essence
that gives the drink its aroma and a bit of its colour.
This essence is highly aromatic.
It gives our aperitif its typical orange note along with a little bit of colour.
How many bottles do you produce?
We produce between 50,000 and 60,000 bottles of our aperitif.
It sounds like a lot but when you consider that people drink
over a million aperitifs every day worldwide
you realise it's quite a small niche.
A small niche created with heart I would say.
Above all it is about quality and that is a significant advantage.
Peritif is more than a drink.
It's a feeling, a pause, a moment to share.
This is exactly where Rondo and Red Veneziano reveal their best qualities
natural, light and full of South Tyrolian character.
In the end it's important to us to make a natural, genuine
and high quality drink to enjoy with friends.
Well then to a special moment and to natural ingredients.
Cheers!

as the Sabiona Monastery wines
of which is Sabiona Silvana and the Kerner

is very useful in the kitchen, but also in baths to help inflammation.
And now it is time to taste the properties of these herbs.
Now we will go to my farm to prepare a herb pesto which you can taste.
The last step of this exciting trip is Rosie's farmhouse
where she has a very large and rich garden and her shop.
Here with a splendid view of the valley and jovial castle,
Rosie has arranged everything we need to prepare a surprising pesto of wild herbs.
Let's go ladies, it's time to shred.
The right mix of herbs mixed with pumpkin seeds,
high quality extra virgin olive oil and rums and salt
and this pesto on a slice of homemade, naturally leavened bread
successfully completes this tour with Rosie.
Please ladies.
Bike fans get their money's worth in the Ega Valley
and have the opportunity to explore one of the most beautiful biking regions in South Tyrol.
The bike school Egental organizes guided mountain bike and e-bike tours for every level.
For the full biking experience in Val d'Ega,
you don't necessarily have to have your own bike.
Numerous rentals are available to guests and locals.
In addition, everything you could need is available here
at Ivan Riegler's bike store in Nova Ponente.
I have e-bikes with Bosch motors for rental here.
On offer are also fullies or regular bikes to rent
and everything else enthusiastic bikers might want.
T-shirts, cycling goggles and backpacks are all available here.
Since the right equipment has been found,
the next step is to choose a suitable tour.
In Val d'Ega, hobby and passionate cyclists get their money's worth.
The biking region has much to offer.
We ride basically through the entire region.
The bike guide leads you along the correct trails,
so maps are unnecessary.
We also provide information on the surrounding mountains and the local terrain.
The first group is already ready to go.
The bike guide provides information about the tour before the start
and the last participants are soon rounded up.
The first group goes in the direction of the Costa Lunga pass.
The second group has planned a small, more leisurely alpine tour
for this hot summer day.
Together with two bike guides,
they cycle along forest paths and underneath leafy canopies.
Again and again, the bikers are rewarded with lovely mountain vistas
that come into view.
During the ride, the guides always give helpful advice.
So that it's not too dull for the younger participants,
the trails also lead over more challenging routes and stones.
We explain a little bit about how to break,
take turns and keep control of the bike.
We can offer almost anything here,
from trails with a difficulty rating of five and six
to courses suited for beginners.
